name: slb
description: Simultaneous Launch Button - Two-person rule for destructive commands in multi-agent workflows. Risk-tiered classification, command hash binding, 5 execution gates, client-side execution with environment inheritance. Go CLI.
capabilities: Classifies commands into CRITICAL, DANGEROUS, CAUTION, and SAFE tiers
Requires peer approval for destructive commands before execution
Binds commands to SHA-256 hashes to prevent tampering
Captures rollback state before executing risky commands
Integrates with Claude Code via PreToolUse hook
Provides real-time event streaming for multi-agent workflows
author: Dicklesworthstone
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill. All static findings are false positives - the flagged patterns (rm -rf, DROP TABLE, terraform destroy, etc.) are documented examples of dangerous commands that SLB is designed to PROTECT against, not malicious code. The skill-report.json from the same scan batch correctly assessed this as 'safe'. No executable code exists in this skill.
valueStatement: AI agents can accidentally run destructive commands that cause irreversible damage. This skill integrates the Simultaneous Launch Button tool to implement a two-person rule, requiring peer approval before executing risky commands like rm, git push --force, or DROP TABLE.
userTitle: Add safety gates for destructive commands
limitations: Does not execute commands itself - integrates with external Go CLI tool
Requires manual installation of the SLB Go binary
Does not prevent commands outside of the SLB workflow

useCases.title: Protect production deployments
useCases.description: Require team approval before running kubectl delete or terraform destroy in production environments

useCases.title: Prevent AI data loss
useCases.description: Add safety gates so multiple AI agents must approve rm, DROP TABLE, or git reset --hard commands

useCases.title: Audit dangerous operations
useCases.description: Log all destructive commands and require human or peer AI review before execution

promptTemplates.title: Check command safety
promptTemplates.scenario: Verify if a command requires approval
promptTemplates.prompt: Check what tier this command falls under using SLB patterns test: <command>

promptTemplates.title: Request approval
promptTemplates.scenario: Submit a command for peer review
promptTemplates.prompt: Run slb request "<command>" --reason "<reason>" and show me the request ID

promptTemplates.title: Approve request
promptTemplates.scenario: Review and approve a pending request
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me all pending SLB requests with slb pending --all-projects, then review request <request-id>

promptTemplates.title: Execute safely
promptTemplates.scenario: Run a command through the approval workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Run slb run "<command>" --reason "<reason>" --session-id <session-id> and wait for approval

bestPractices: Initialize SLB in each project directory before running AI agents
Use different AI models for requester and reviewer when possible
Configure rollback capture for CRITICAL tier commands
antiPatterns: Running destructive commands outside the SLB workflow
Self-approving requests without actual peer review

faq.question: Which AI tools work with SLB?
faq.answer: SLB integrates with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through hook installations and manual commands

faq.question: What happens if the daemon is unreachable?
faq.answer: SLB falls back to file-based polling. Dangerous commands are blocked until the daemon is available

faq.question: Can I bypass approval in emergencies?
faq.answer: Yes, use emergency-execute with hash acknowledgment. All bypasses are extensively logged

faq.question: Is my data sent to external servers?
faq.answer: No. SLB runs locally and commands execute in your shell environment with full environment inheritance

faq.question: What commands are always blocked?
faq.answer: Commands matching CRITICAL patterns like rm -rf /, DROP DATABASE, and terraform destroy require 2+ approvals

faq.question: How does SLB compare to similar tools?
faq.answer: Unlike git protection or container guards, SLB works across all command types and supports multi-agent peer review
